title: [lichen_util.h:66-79] secure_zero word-aligned path has subtle aliasing concern
description:
Gap found: secure_zero implementation not fully analyzed in Round 1.

Why this was missed: Round 1 found NULL return issue but not the algorithm.

The actual bug: Lines 66-79 implement word-aligned zeroing. The cast (uintptr_t)p on line 66 checks alignment, but the while loop (73-79) uses __builtin_memcpy to a volatile pointer. The pattern is:
1. Cast volatile uint8_t* to void* for memcpy destination
2. memcpy writes zero_word
3. Read-back via volatile to prevent optimization

The concern: The memcpy destination is (void*)p where p is volatile uint8_t*. Casting away volatile is implementation-defined behavior per C standard. While __builtin_memcpy likely treats the destination as volatile due to the subsequent read-back, a very aggressive optimizer could theoretically reorder or elide the memcpy since the destination type no longer carries volatile. The read-back forces observable behavior but occurs after the write. Consider using volatile_memcpy or explicit volatile stores. Priority 3 because current compilers respect this pattern.
close_reason: Fixed: Removed the cast-away-volatile pattern that caused the aliasing concern. The previous implementation cast volatile uint8_t* to void* for memcpy, which is technically UB. Now uses volatile unsigned long* directly for word-sized writes, preserving volatile semantics throughout and avoiding any strict aliasing issues.
